document.title: Legal Guardianship and Estate — UNJSPF Booklet
document.revision: UNJSPF Booklet: Legal Guardianship and Estate

chunks.part: Terms and Acronyms
chunks.heading: What do the terms Participant, Retiree, Beneficiary, Guardian, and UNJSPF mean in this booklet?
chunks.text: Participant: refers to an active staff member contributing to the Fund in accordance with articles 21 and 25 of the Regulations of the Fund. Retiree: refers to a participant who has officially separated from the service of his/her employing organization and is in receipt of a periodic benefit payment from the Fund. Beneficiary: refers to a person entitled to a survivor's benefit (widow/widower, child, disabled child, divorced surviving spouse or secondary dependant). Guardian: refers to a third party appointed by the appropriate judicial authority in the retiree's or beneficiary's country of residence to manage his/her financial and/or pension affairs. UNJSPF: United Nations Joint Staff Pension Fund, also referred to as "the Fund".

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: When does the Fund require the appointment of a legal guardian for a retiree or adult beneficiary?
chunks.text: As a general rule, where a retiree or adult beneficiary is unable to manage his/her affairs due to ill health and/or infirmity emanating from advanced age, the Fund requires the appointment of a legal guardian to act on behalf of the retiree or beneficiary. The Fund requires that retirees and beneficiaries affix an original signature on all forms, and changes to information can be made only based on a document carrying the date, pension/retirement number, full name, and original signature matching the one on file. These requirements are intended to ensure benefits are paid to the right person and minimize the risk of fraud, balanced against the Fund's fiduciary obligation to all participants and beneficiaries.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: What documentation is required by the Fund to recognize a legal guardian appointed to act on behalf of a retiree or adult beneficiary?
chunks.text: The guardian must be appointed by the appropriate judicial authority in the retiree's or beneficiary's country of residence, and must submit to the Fund: (a) a full copy of the judicial decision appointing him/her as guardian, with a mandate that includes management of the retiree's or beneficiary's financial and/or pension-related affairs; (b) the contact details of each legally appointed guardian; (c) a copy of the passport or other official photo-ID of each guardian; (d) if the guardian is an organization, agency or trust, the name of the authorized signing employee/trustee and their identification document; (e) all documents in English or French, or accompanied by a professional translation.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: Does the Fund only recognize court-appointed legal guardians or does it also recognize appointments under other protection regimes?
chunks.text: Although this booklet uses the term "guardian" generically, it covers court appointments under different types of protection regimes available under different jurisdictions, including tutorship, conservatorship and curatorship.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: Does the Fund recognize appointments that are not made by a court but registered by a governmental authority?
chunks.text: The Fund's policy is that the guardian must be appointed by the appropriate judicial authority in the country of residence. However, where national law provides alternative arrangements overseen or approved by a governmental authority (e.g., registered lasting or enduring powers of attorney in the UK, Ireland, and Canada/Quebec, managed by a central authority such as the Office of the Public Guardian or Office of Wards of Court), such appointment is also recognized. The third party must submit a full copy of the registering document, in English or French or with a professional translation.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: Does the Fund accept third parties appointed under a power of attorney or affidavit?
chunks.text: The Fund does not recognize appointments of third parties by means of powers of attorney or affidavits, even if the affidavits were approved by a national court.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: What are the powers of the legal guardian acting on behalf of a retiree or beneficiary in relation to UNJSPF benefits?
chunks.text: A guardian must act within the scope of his/her judicial appointment, and can sign the Fund's forms in areas where authorized and correspond with the Fund. A UNJSPF benefit can only be paid to a bank account in the name of the retiree or beneficiary; a guardian without access to that account should open a guardianship/trust account in the name of or on behalf of the retiree/beneficiary, or a joint account with the guardian, and submit the Payment Instruction Form with a bank statement or letter confirming the account name(s).

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: What information is required by the Fund on forms and correspondence sent on behalf of the retiree or beneficiary?
chunks.text: Forms or letters sent by the guardian must include the full name of the retiree or beneficiary, his/her retirement number, the full name and title of the guardian (i.e. guardian of [name]), and the date.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: What should be done if the appointment of the legal guardian has been cancelled, amended, or replaced?
chunks.text: The guardian must send to the Fund immediately any decisions or documents which amend or cancel his/her appointment.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: What if the legal guardian has been appointed on a temporary basis or for a limited duration?
chunks.text: The same documentation requirements apply. It is the guardian's obligation to promptly send any decisions that extend, amend, or cancel the appointment, or appoint a permanent guardian. If a court decision does not specify a length, the temporary guardian must inform the Fund when proceedings for a permanent guardian are expected and send resulting decisions; the Fund will normally review the appointment six months after the initial guardianship to determine whether payment should be suspended.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: Under what circumstances does the Fund accept a thumbprint in lieu of an original signature?
chunks.text: Where a retiree or beneficiary can no longer sign documents due to ill health, the Fund accepts an authenticated original thumbprint instead of a signature. The thumbprint must be authenticated by an official of the former employing organization, a UNJSPF Official, a Government Official, a Notary Public, or an attending physician, who affixes their full name, title, date, stamp/seal, license/index number if applicable, and original signature. The first time this is used, the Fund must also receive a medical certificate (in English or French, or translated) explaining the medical condition preventing signature. This arrangement cannot be used to amend payment instructions or the "Designation of recipient of residual settlement" (UNJSPF A/2) form, and the Fund recommends starting guardianship proceedings during this period.

chunks.part: Retirees and adult beneficiaries unable to manage their affairs
chunks.heading: What if a retiree or beneficiary can still sign documents, but the signature differs notably from the one on file due to the passage of time?
chunks.text: The Fund will request the signature be duly authenticated by an official of the former employing organization, a UNJSPF Official, a Government Official, or a Notary Public, following the same authentication requirements as for a thumbprint. Future signatures must match the newly authenticated one, and the Fund recommends starting guardianship proceedings during this period.

chunks.part: Legal guardians for beneficiaries of child benefits under 21 or disabled
chunks.heading: How is a UNJSPF child benefit paid when the child is a minor or disabled, and who can be a legal guardian?
chunks.text: A UNJSPF benefit payable to the child of a former participant or retiree, when the child is under 21 or disabled, is usually paid on the child's behalf to the former participant or retiree, per UNJSPF Administrative Rule J.2(e). If the child resides with a parent who is not the former participant/retiree, payment is made to that parent. When the parents are no longer alive or the child is in the custody of a third party, the child's benefit is paid to a legal guardian appointed for the child by the appropriate judicial authority in the child's country of residence. The benefit can only be paid to a guardianship/trust account in the name of or on behalf of the child, an account in the child's name, or a joint account with the legal guardian. When a minor child turns 16, or a disabled child can manage his/her own financial affairs, the benefit can be remitted directly to him/her.

chunks.part: Legal guardians for beneficiaries of child benefits under 21 or disabled
chunks.heading: What documentation is required by the Fund to recognize a legal guardian of a minor or disabled child beneficiary?
chunks.text: The third party must submit: (a) a full copy of the judicial decision appointing a guardian for the child in the child's country of residence; (b) contact details of each legal guardian; (c) a copy of the passport or other official photo-ID of each guardian; (d) a UNJSPF Payment Instruction Form signed by the guardian, stating the child's name, the guardianship/trust or joint account details, and the guardian's name; (e) a bank statement or letter confirming the account name(s); (f) all documents in English or French, or with a professional translation.

chunks.part: Payment to the estate of a late participant or retiree
chunks.heading: When does the Fund make a payment to the estate of a late participant or retiree?
chunks.text: Upon death, the Fund first determines whether a survivor's benefit is payable to a widow(er), divorced surviving spouse, children, or secondary dependants; as long as such a benefit is payable, no other payments (e.g. a residual settlement) are due. If there is no survivor's benefit payable, the Fund examines entitlement to a residual settlement under Article 38 of the UNJSPF Regulations, payable only if the total benefits paid to and on account of the late participant/retiree (including any survivor's benefit) is less than their own contributions to the Fund. The difference is paid to the person(s)/institution designated on the "Designation of recipient of residual settlement" (UNJSPF A/2) form as a one-time lump sum; if there is no such form on file, the amount is paid to the estate. Note that there is no residual settlement due to the estate if the retiree had elected, upon separation, the maximum lump-sum commutation allowed (the greater of the actuarial equivalent of 1/3 of the benefit or total contributions with compound interest) plus a reduced monthly pension.

chunks.part: Payment to the estate of a late participant or retiree
chunks.heading: What documents must the Fund receive in order to make a payment to the estate of a late participant or retiree?
chunks.text: The Fund requires: (a) a full copy of the judicial decision appointing an administrator or executor of the estate; (b) contact details of each legally appointed administrator/executor; (c) a copy of the passport or other official photo-ID of each; (d) normally, a copy of a bank statement or letter confirming the name(s) on an estate or guardianship/trust bank account opened by the executor/administrator; (e) the UNJSPF Payment Instruction Form signed by the executor(s)/administrator(s) — if payment is requested in the name of only one of multiple appointees, the others' authorization is required; (f) all documents in English or French, or with a professional translation.

chunks.part: Payment to the estate of a late participant or retiree
chunks.heading: Can the Fund recover overpayments from the estate of the late participant, retiree or beneficiary?
chunks.text: Yes. The Fund can recover from the estate of a late participant, retiree or beneficiary, or from benefits payable to survivors, any erroneous payments or overpayments made, under Article 43 of the UNJSPF Regulations and UNJSPF Administrative Rule J.9.

chunks.part: Relevant provisions of the UNJSPF Regulations and Administrative Rules
chunks.heading: What does Article 36 (Child's Benefit) of the UNJSPF Regulations provide?
chunks.text: (a) A child's benefit shall, subject to (b) and (c) below, be payable for each child of a participant who is entitled to a retirement, early retirement or disability benefit or who has died in service, while the child remains under the age of 21. (b) A benefit shall be payable for a child who is over the age of 21 if the child is found by the Board to have been incapacitated by illness or injury for substantial gainful employment: (i) on reaching the age of 21, if immediately prior thereto a child's benefit was payable; or (ii) at the time of the death in service or entitlement to a benefit of the participant. A benefit payable as above shall continue for as long as the child remains incapacitated. (c) A child's benefit shall, notwithstanding (a) above, not become payable, if the participant has chosen an early retirement benefit, until the participant dies or reaches the normal retirement age, except to a child under the age of 21 found by the Board to be disabled.

chunks.part: Relevant provisions of the UNJSPF Regulations and Administrative Rules
chunks.heading: What does Article 38 (Residual Settlement) of the UNJSPF Regulations provide?
chunks.text: (a) A residual settlement shall be payable if, upon the death of a participant and the exhaustion, as the case may be, of any entitlements due under these Regulations to his or her survivors, the total amount of the benefits paid to and on account of the participant is less than the participant's own contributions. (b) The settlement shall be payable to a beneficiary designated by the participant and alive when the payment is due; failing such beneficiary, the settlement shall be paid to the estate of the participant. (c) The settlement shall consist of the participant's own contributions at the date of his or her separation or death in service, reduced by the total amount of the benefits paid to and on account of the participant.

chunks.part: Relevant provisions of the UNJSPF Regulations and Administrative Rules
chunks.heading: What does Article 43 (Recovery of Indebtedness to the Fund) of the UNJSPF Regulations provide?
chunks.text: The Board may deduct from any benefit payable under these Regulations to a participant, or on his or her account, the amount of any indebtedness to the Fund by the participant or by any beneficiary or third person to whom payment has been made otherwise than in accordance with these Regulations, including interest and costs, where appropriate.

chunks.part: Relevant provisions of the UNJSPF Regulations and Administrative Rules
chunks.heading: What does Administrative Rules Section I (Entitlement to Benefits) provide?
chunks.text: I.1 Entitlement to a benefit shall, subject to rules I.3 and I.4 below but without further action by an organ of the Fund other than as may be required to determine eligibility for a disability or incapacitated child's benefit under these Rules, vest in a participant and in the child of a participant on the day succeeding the last day of contributory service; it shall vest in the widow, widower, secondary dependant, designated beneficiaries or estate of a participant on the day succeeding the day of the participant's death in service, and on the first day of the month succeeding the death if the participant died while in receipt of a periodic benefit. I.2 Entitlement to a benefit under article 36(a) of the Regulations shall continue to the end of the month in which the child reaches the age of twenty-one.

chunks.part: Relevant provisions of the UNJSPF Regulations and Administrative Rules
chunks.heading: What do Administrative Rules J.2(e) and J.9(a) (Computation and Payment of Benefits) provide?
chunks.text: J.2(e) Benefits payable under the Regulations to the children of a participant shall, unless there are exceptional circumstances, be paid on their behalf to the participant and, upon the participant's death, to the surviving parent or legal guardian of each child, in accordance, mutatis mutandis, with (a), (b), (c) and (d) above. J.9(a) Any payments made by the Fund to a participant, beneficiary of a participant or third person otherwise than in accordance with the Regulations of the Fund may be deducted from any future benefits payable to or on account of the participant under these Regulations or may be recovered directly from the person or estate of the person to whom any such payments were made. The Chief Executive Officer may, where such an overpayment had been attributable to the submission of incorrect information to the Fund, recover interest, as well as administrative costs of 10 per cent of the overpayment.
